_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against WHO-HAEM4 criteria for erythrocytosis, ELN recommendations, and published literature on secondary polycythemia etiology, diagnosis, and management. The condition is distinguished from primary polycythemia (polycythemia vera) by the absence of JAK2/CALR/MPL mutations and the presence of an identifiable cause.

h1Title: Secondary Polycythemia: Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Secondary polycythemia is an erythrocytosis driven by increased erythropoietin (EPO) production in response to chronic tissue hypoxia or, less commonly, by EPO-secreting tumors — it is not a primary bone marrow disorder like polycythemia vera. The most common cause is chronic hypoxemia from chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), sleep apnea, or living at high altitude, where the kidneys detect low oxygen tension and increase EPO production to stimulate red blood cell production. Hemoglobin and hematocrit rise above normal limits (hemoglobin above 18.5 g/dL in men, 16.5 g/dL in women; hematocrit above 50% in men, 45% in women) as a compensatory response. Unlike polycythemia vera, secondary polycythemia does not carry an inherent risk of thrombosis from the polycythemia alone — the thrombotic risk is determined by the underlying condition (COPD, sleep apnea) rather than the elevated blood counts.

keyStats.prevalence: Secondary polycythemia is common — occurs in 10–15% of COPD patients; approximately 5–10% of obstructive sleep apnea patients develop polycythemia; high-altitude polycythemia affects over 100 million people globally
keyStats.demographics: More common in males (2:1 ratio); age distribution follows the underlying cause (COPD patients in 60s–70s; sleep apnea patients in 40s–60s; high-altitude residents at any age)
keyStats.avgAge: Median age varies by cause: COPD-related polycythemia median age 65–70 years; sleep apnea-related median age 50–55 years; congenital polycythemia presents in adolescence or early adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: High-altitude polycythemia (excess erythrocytosis) is the most common form globally, affecting populations in the Andes, Tibetan plateau, and Ethiopian highlands; estimated 100–150 million people affected

definition: Secondary polycythemia (ICD-10: D75.1) is an erythrocytosis characterized by an elevated red blood cell mass and hemoglobin/hematocrit above normal values due to increased erythropoietin (EPO) production. The elevated EPO is a compensatory response to chronic hypoxia or a consequence of EPO-secreting tumors — unlike polycythemia vera (PV), where the bone marrow produces red cells autonomously without EPO stimulation. The WHO diagnostic threshold for erythrocytosis is hemoglobin above 185 g/L (18.5 g/dL) and hematocrit above 52% in men, and above 165 g/L (16.5 g/dL) and 48% in women. The causes are divided into three categories: appropriate EPO-driven erythrocytosis (chronic hypoxemia from COPD, sleep apnea, cyanotic heart disease, high altitude), inappropriate EPO secretion (renal tumors, hepatocellular carcinoma, cerebellar hemangioblastoma), and rare congenital mutations affecting the hypoxia-sensing pathway (VHL, EGLN1/PHD2, EPOR). Treatment focuses on managing the underlying cause; phlebotomy is reserved for symptomatic hyperviscosity or pre-operative reduction of hematocrit. Clinical assessment by a specialist is recommended for confirmation and management.

typesClassification.type: Appropriate hypoxic secondary polycythemia — COPD and chronic hypoxemia
typesClassification.description: Chronic hypoxemia from COPD causes the kidneys to increase EPO production, stimulating red cell production as a compensatory mechanism to deliver more oxygen to tissues. This is the most common form in developed countries. Hemoglobin elevation is often modest (17–19 g/dL) but may be severe in advanced COPD with chronic hypoxemia.

Repeated episodes of nocturnal hypoxia during sleep cause chronic EPO elevation and erythrocytosis. The severity of polycythemia correlates with the severity of OSA (measured by AHI score) and the degree of nocturnal desaturation. CPAP treatment reduces EPO levels and hematocrit.

Residents of high-altitude regions (>2,500 meters) develop appropriate polycythemia as an adaptive response to reduced barometric pressure and lower arterial oxygen tension. Excess erythrocytosis is defined as hemoglobin above 21 g/dL (men) or 19 g/dL (women) at high altitude. Tibetan highlanders have a genetic adaptation that blunts EPO response, preventing the extreme polycythemia seen in Andean populations.

Children and adults with cyanotic heart disease (right-to-left cardiac shunts) have systemic arterial hypoxemia from birth, causing chronic EPO-driven erythrocytosis. Hematocrit may reach 60–70% in severe cases. Management involves careful phlebotomy to maintain hematocrit in the 55–65% range, as higher values increase stroke risk.

Rare tumors that secrete EPO ectopically cause erythrocytosis independent of oxygen sensing: clear cell renal carcinoma (most common), hepatocellular carcinoma, cerebellar hemangioblastoma, uterine leiomyoma, and pheochromocytoma. Erythrocytosis resolves upon tumor resection. EPO levels are markedly elevated (>100 mIU/mL) in paraneoplastic cases.

Rare inherited disorders causing isolated erythrocytosis without evidence of hypoxia or EPO-secreting tumors. VHL mutations cause Chuvash polycythemia (high prevalence in Russian Chuvash population); PHD2 mutations cause gain-of-function that mimics hypoxia sensing; EPOR mutations cause constitutive EPO receptor activation. Generally benign but associated with increased thrombotic risk in some forms.

primarySymptoms: Headache and fullness in the head, particularly in the morning — elevated hematocrit increases blood viscosity, and cerebral blood flow is affected; morning headache is a classic symptom of polycythemia and may improve with phlebotomy.
Dizziness and lightheadedness, especially when standing — high hematocrit increases blood viscosity and can cause orthostatic symptoms; hyperviscosity itself causes reduced cerebral perfusion.

Fatigue and somnolence, disproportionate to the degree of anemia — while polycythemia increases oxygen-carrying capacity in theory, the increased blood viscosity reduces microcirculatory flow and tissue perfusion efficiency, paradoxically worsening fatigue.

Shortness of breath, particularly on exertion — in COPD-related polycythemia, dyspnea is primarily due to the underlying lung disease; polycythemia worsens exercise intolerance by increasing blood viscosity and reducing cardiac output.

Plethora (reddish complexion) of the face, hands, and mucous membranes — the characteristic ruddy or cyanotic appearance is caused by the increased red cell mass and, in some cases, persistent hypoxemia giving a bluish-purple hue.
Visual disturbances (blurred vision, diplopia) from retinal vein engorgement and sludging — high hematocrit causes retinal vein distension and may lead to retinal vein thrombosis in severe cases.

Pruritus (itching) after bathing — aquagenic pruritus is more characteristic of polycythemia vera but can also occur in secondary polycythemia from unknown mechanisms, possibly related to histamine release from increased basophils.
Epistaxis (nosebleeds) and easy bruising — in some secondary polycythemia patients, platelet dysfunction and hyperviscosity cause bleeding tendency; coagulation studies may show mixed findings.

Hypertension, particularly in high-altitude polycythemia — elevated hematocrit increases blood viscosity and peripheral resistance, contributing to systemic hypertension; EPO itself may cause vasoconstriction.

Symptoms of the underlying condition (COPD dyspnea, sleep apnea symptoms) — the clinical presentation often reflects both the polycythemia and the primary condition driving it; distinguishing the contribution of each is important.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistently elevated hemoglobin (above 18.5 g/dL in men, 16.5 g/dL in women) on routine blood work, confirmed on repeat testing
Morning headache and dizziness in a patient with known COPD or sleep apnea
Unexplained erythrocytosis in a patient with a known EPO-secreting tumor (renal cell carcinoma, HCC)
Severe erythrocytosis (hemoglobin above 20 g/dL) without JAK2 mutation — strongly suggests secondary rather than primary polycythemia
Family history of polycythemia — may suggest congenital erythrocytosis syndromes
emergencySigns: Acute neurological symptoms (stroke, TIA) in a polycythemic patient — severe polycythemia (hematocrit above 60%) dramatically increases stroke risk, particularly in cyanotic heart disease and high-altitude polycythemia; requires urgent phlebotomy and evaluation
Visual disturbances suggesting retinal vein or artery thrombosis — sudden vision loss, blurred vision, or diplopia in a polycythemic patient warrants urgent ophthalmologic evaluation

Chest pain or signs of myocardial ischemia — hyperviscosity reduces coronary blood flow; acute coronary syndrome in a polycythemic patient requires standard acute coronary treatment plus hematocrit management

Acute bleeding (GI hemorrhage, intracranial hemorrhage) — polycythemic patients may paradoxically have bleeding complications from platelet dysfunction and coagulation abnormalities despite high platelet counts; requires urgent evaluation

Symptoms of hyperviscosity syndrome (confusion, neurologic deficits, visual changes, epistaxis) — when hematocrit rises above 55–60%, symptoms of hyperviscosity become prominent and may require urgent phlebotomy

causes.description: COPD causes chronic alveolar hypoxemia and subsequent arterial hypoxemia, stimulating renal EPO production. The degree of polycythemia correlates with the severity of hypoxemia (PaO2 below 60 mmHg, SaO2 below 88% chronically) and the duration of exposure. COPD patients with chronic hypoxemia treated with long-term oxygen therapy have lower hemoglobin than those without supplemental oxygen — LTOT reduces EPO stimulation and modestly reduces hematocrit.

causes.cause: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) with chronic nocturnal hypoxia
causes.description: Repeated nocturnal desaturation during sleep (episodes below 90% SaO2, lasting minutes at a time) causes cumulative hypoxia exposure that stimulates EPO production over months to years. The severity of nocturnal desaturation (measured by nadir SpO2 and cumulative time below 90%) correlates with the degree of erythrocytosis. CPAP treatment reduces EPO levels within weeks.

At altitudes above 2,500 meters, reduced barometric pressure lowers the partial pressure of oxygen in inspired air, causing arterial hypoxemia. The kidneys respond to this chronic hypoxemia by increasing EPO production, driving erythrocytosis. Long-term residents of high altitude may develop excessive erythrocytosis (>21 g/dL hemoglobin) with significant hyperviscosity and increased thrombotic risk.

Right-to-left cardiac shunts bypass the lungs, delivering partially deoxygenated blood to the systemic circulation. This chronic arterial hypoxemia from birth causes sustained EPO elevation. Patients may have hematocrits of 60–70%, with increased risk of stroke, brain abscess, and bleeding complications. Phlebotomy targets are individualized based on symptoms and risk.

Clear cell renal carcinoma is the most common tumor causing paraneoplastic EPO secretion — approximately 5–10% of clear cell RCC patients have elevated EPO levels and clinical erythrocytosis. Other tumors include hepatocellular carcinoma, cerebellar hemangioblastoma, uterine leiomyoma, and pheochromocytoma. Erythrocytosis resolves with tumor resection or control.

Smoking causes an apparent polycythemia through plasma volume contraction — smoking increases carboxyhemoglobin (which cannot carry oxygen), causing tissue hypoxia and stimulating EPO despite normal or elevated RBC mass. The measured hemoglobin is elevated but the true red cell mass may be normal (relative or spurious polycythemia). Smoking cessation allows plasma volume to normalize and hemoglobin to return to baseline.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic hypoxemia (PaO2 below 60 mmHg, SaO2 below 88%)
riskFactors.description: The degree of chronic hypoxemia is the primary driver of secondary polycythemia. Patients with COPD, OSA, or living at high altitude with significant nocturnal or continuous hypoxemia are at risk. Long-term oxygen therapy reduces EPO-driven erythrocytosis in COPD.

Males have higher baseline hemoglobin than females and are more frequently affected by polycythemia — the higher testosterone in males stimulates erythropoiesis, and the absence of menstrual iron loss allows higher iron stores to support greater red cell production.

Smoking causes both true and relative polycythemia — it induces hypoxemia through carbon monoxide binding to hemoglobin, and the associated plasma volume contraction creates spurious elevation of hemoglobin and hematocrit. Smoking cessation leads to normalization of hematocrit in many cases.

Prolonged residence at high altitude causes appropriate polycythemia through the hypoxic stimulus for EPO production. The degree of polycythemia is proportional to altitude and duration of exposure. Populations that have lived at high altitude for generations have adapted polycythemia responses (genetic vs. acquired).

Iron supplementation in patients with polycythemia and iron sufficiency may exacerbate erythrocytosis by providing the iron needed for accelerated red cell production. Patients with secondary polycythemia should avoid iron supplements unless iron deficiency is documented.

Diuretics and dehydration cause plasma volume contraction, elevating hemoglobin and hematocrit measurements (relative polycythemia). This may contribute to or exacerbate true polycythemia in at-risk patients. Discontinuing diuretics and rehydrating can clarify whether the elevated hematocrit is true or spurious.

affectedDemographics: Secondary polycythemia from COPD is most common in men aged 60–75 years with moderate-to-severe COPD; the prevalence of polycythemia in COPD patients with chronic hypoxemia is approximately 10–15%
Sleep apnea-related polycythemia occurs equally in men and women, typically in middle-aged adults (45–65 years) with moderate-to-severe OSA
High-altitude polycythemia affects populations living above 2,500 meters — the Andean, Tibetan, and Ethiopian highlands have the highest prevalence; estimated 100–150 million people affected globally
Paraneoplastic erythrocytosis from renal cell carcinoma occurs in older adults (median age 65) with known or occult malignancy
Congenital erythrocytosis is rare (estimated prevalence 1:50,000 to 1:100,000) and presents in adolescence or early adulthood
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of secondary polycythemia begins with confirming true erythrocytosis (elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit on at least two occasions) and then distinguishing primary from secondary polycythemia. The key test is JAK2 V617F and exon 12 mutation testing — absence of these mutations strongly supports secondary polycythemia rather than PV. EPO levels are central to the differentiation: in secondary polycythemia, EPO is elevated (appropriate hypoxic EPO response) or very high (paraneoplastic EPO secretion), while in PV, EPO is typically suppressed (low-normal or below the reference range). If EPO is elevated, evaluation for causes of hypoxemia (ABG, pulse oximetry, sleep study) and EPO-secreting tumors (abdominal imaging, renal imaging) is indicated. Serum ferritin and iron studies help characterize iron status — iron deficiency can mask more severe erythrocytosis and becomes apparent after iron repletion. Serum erythropoietin level, arterial blood gases, carboxyhemoglobin (in smokers), abdominal imaging, and carboxyhemoglobin levels guide the differential diagnosis. Clinical assessment by a specialist is recommended for confirmation and management.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with hemoglobin and hematocrit (confirmatory)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms erythrocytosis: hemoglobin above 185 g/L (18.5 g/dL) and hematocrit above 52% in men; above 165 g/L (16.5 g/dL) and 48% in women. Repeat testing on separate occasions confirms persistent elevation and excludes transient causes (dehydration).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within hours. Confirm elevation on at least two occasions before pursuing further workup.

diagnosticTests.test: JAK2 V617F mutation and exon 12 testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: The critical test to distinguish secondary polycythemia from polycythemia vera. JAK2 V617F is present in over 95% of PV cases; JAK2 exon 12 mutations account for most remaining PV cases. Absence of JAK2 mutations strongly suggests secondary polycythemia rather than PV. CALR and MPL mutations (seen in essential thrombocythemia and primary myelofibrosis) are typically absent in PV.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for molecular testing; results in 5–10 days. JAK2 V617F can be run as a rapid assay within 24–48 hours if needed for urgent decision-making.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates primary from secondary polycythemia. In secondary polycythemia (appropriate), EPO is elevated (above the upper limit of normal); in PV (primary autonomous erythrocytosis), EPO is suppressed (low-normal or below the lower limit of normal). Very high EPO levels (>100–200 mIU/mL) suggest paraneoplastic EPO secretion from a tumor.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1–2 days. EPO levels must be interpreted in the context of hemoglobin — a 'normal' EPO in a patient with significant erythrocytosis is actually inappropriately low.

diagnosticTests.test: Arterial blood gases (ABG) and oxygen saturation
diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures PaO2, PaCO2, pH, and SaO2 to identify chronic hypoxemia as the driver of secondary polycythemia. A PaO2 below 60 mmHg or SaO2 below 88% on room air confirms tissue hypoxia driving EPO production. ABG can be compared to pulse oximetry readings — elevated carboxyhemoglobin in smokers lowers pulse ox readings and causes apparent hypoxemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Arterial blood draw (from radial artery); results within 30 minutes. May cause brief discomfort at the puncture site.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses obstructive sleep apnea as a cause of secondary polycythemia — the sleep study measures apnea-hypopnea index (AHI), oxygen desaturation index (ODI), and cumulative time with SaO2 below 90%. An AHI above 5 events/hour and significant nocturnal desaturation support OSA as the cause of polycythemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Overnight attended sleep study in a sleep laboratory; home sleep apnea testing is a less comprehensive alternative. Results available within 1–2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies EPO-secreting tumors (renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, hemangioblastoma) as a cause of paraneoplastic erythrocytosis. Also rules out splenomegaly (seen in PV) and assesses for renal cysts or other renal abnormalities that might cause EPO secretion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT scan with IV contrast; takes 15–20 minutes. Requires fasting for 4 hours before the scan. Risks include contrast allergy and contrast-induced nephropathy.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum ferritin, iron, TIBC, transferrin saturation
diagnosticTests.purpose: Iron status assessment — iron deficiency can mask more severe erythrocytosis by limiting red cell production; when iron deficiency is corrected, the true degree of erythrocytosis may become apparent. Iron deficiency is common in COPD patients and may complicate the interpretation of hemoglobin levels. Iron repletion in iron-deficient polycythemic patients may worsen hematocrit.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1–2 days. Iron deficiency is suggested by low ferritin (<30 ng/mL), low serum iron, elevated TIBC, and low transferrin saturation.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of secondary polycythemia targets the underlying cause and reduces thrombotic risk through hematocrit control. The first and most critical step is treating the underlying condition: hypoxia-driven polycythemia requires supplemental oxygen and correction of hypoxemia with continuous or nocturnal oxygen therapy, particularly in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Sleep-disordered breathing (obstructive sleep apnea) treated with CPAP reduces erythrocytosis in many patients. Phlebotomy is used to maintain hematocrit below 45 percent in men and below 42 percent in women, reducing thrombotic risk. Low-dose aspirin 75 to 100 mg daily is added for thrombotic risk reduction in patients without contraindications. For patients with erythrocytosis driven by renal disease, appropriate management of renal function and avoidance of erythropoiesis-stimulating agents reduces hematocrit. EPO-secreting tumors require tumor-directed therapy (surgery, chemotherapy, or radiotherapy) to eliminate the source of excess EPO production. Cytoreductive therapy with hydroxyurea is considered in high-risk secondary polycythemia with prior thrombosis, particularly when the polycythemia is difficult to control with phlebotomy alone. Iron deficiency induced by phlebotomy should not be replaced until hematocrit is controlled, as iron supplementation drives additional erythrocytosis. Long-term monitoring with regular hematocrit checks and assessment of thrombotic events guides ongoing management. Patient education on hydration and early presentation for neurological symptoms suggestive of thrombosis is important.

medicalTreatments.name: Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) for COPD-related polycythemia
medicalTreatments.description: Supplemental oxygen at 2–3 liters per minute via nasal cannula for at least 15 hours daily (ideally continuous) in COPD patients with chronic hypoxemia (PaO2 below 55 mmHg or SaO2 below 88%). LTOT is the most effective treatment for COPD-related polycythemia — it reduces the hypoxic stimulus for EPO production, lowers hemoglobin and hematocrit over months, and improves survival. Target is PaO2 above 60 mmHg (SaO2 above 90%).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: LTOT reduces hematocrit by 3–5% over 3–6 months and significantly improves survival in COPD patients with chronic hypoxemia. It is the single most important therapeutic intervention for polycythemia secondary to COPD.

medicalTreatments.name: CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) for OSA-related polycythemia
medicalTreatments.description: CPAP is the standard treatment for moderate-to-severe obstructive sleep apnea. CPAP eliminates nocturnal hypoxemia by maintaining positive airway pressure during sleep, removing the stimulus for EPO production. CPAP reduces serum EPO levels and hematocrit within 4–12 weeks of effective treatment. Adherence is the primary determinant of effectiveness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective CPAP use reduces hematocrit by 3–6% and improves sleep quality, daytime somnolence, and cardiovascular outcomes. Adherence rates (CPAP used 4+ hours per night) are approximately 50–60%; interventions to improve adherence (education, mask fitting, pressure titration) improve outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Phlebotomy (therapeutic)
medicalTreatments.description: Removal of blood to reduce hematocrit and alleviate hyperviscosity symptoms. Typically 250–500 mL per session, repeated until hematocrit falls to target (typically 45–50% in secondary polycythemia). Phlebotomy is combined with iron deficiency induction (withholding iron supplements) to prevent rapid rebound of hematocrit from iron being available for new RBC production.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid relief of hyperviscosity symptoms (headache, dizziness, visual disturbances) within hours to days of phlebotomy. Target hematocrit of 45–50% reduces symptom burden without causing symptomatic anemia. Rebound erythrocytosis can occur if iron stores are replete — iron restriction is important between phlebotomy sessions.

medicalTreatments.name: Desferrioxamine (iron chelation) — not typically used
medicalTreatments.description: Iron chelation is not a standard treatment for secondary polycythemia. However, in rare cases where repeated phlebotomy is contraindicated and iron overload from transfusion (in cyanotic heart disease) is a concern, iron chelation may be considered.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Not standard treatment for secondary polycythemia.

medicalTreatments.name: Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors
medicalTreatments.description: ACE inhibitors have been reported to reduce hematocrit in polycythemic patients with cyanotic heart disease and in renal transplant recipients with post-transplant erythrocytosis. The mechanism may involve interference with the renin-angiotensin system's role in erythropoiesis (ACE inhibitors reduce EPO production). May be considered as adjunctive treatment in selected patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest hematocrit reduction (2–5%) in selected patients with polycythemia; not a primary treatment. May be considered in patients with concurrent hypertension or cardiac dysfunction who benefit from ACE inhibitor therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Cytoreductive therapy (hydroxyurea) — rarely indicated
medicalTreatments.description: Hydroxyurea is not indicated for secondary polycythemia because the underlying problem is not a primary bone marrow disorder. However, in patients with severe symptomatic polycythemia refractory to phlebotomy and underlying condition management, and in whom a primary myeloproliferative disorder is suspected but not confirmed, hydroxyurea may be considered.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Not a standard treatment for secondary polycythemia — reserved for unusual cases refractory to all other approaches.

surgicalOptions.name: Renal tumor resection (for paraneoplastic erythrocytosis)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of an EPO-secreting renal cell carcinoma or other renal tumor eliminates the source of inappropriate EPO secretion. Erythrocytosis resolves within weeks to months of successful tumor resection.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complete resolution of erythrocytosis in over 80% of patients after complete tumor resection. Erythrocytosis may recur if the tumor recurs or metastasizes.

surgicalOptions.name: Bilateral lung volume reduction surgery or transplant (for severe COPD)
surgicalOptions.description: In select patients with advanced emphysema, lung volume reduction surgery or lung transplantation can improve oxygenation, reduce hypoxemia, and thereby reduce the stimulus for polycythemia. Reserved for advanced disease meeting specific surgical criteria.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Variable — lung volume reduction surgery can improve PaO2 by 5–15 mmHg in selected patients, reducing hypoxemia and modestly reducing hematocrit. Lung transplant offers the most dramatic improvement in oxygenation but carries significant surgical risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: High-altitude relocation
alternativeTreatments.description: For patients with severe high-altitude polycythemia (excess erythrocytosis), relocating to lower altitude (below 1,500 meters) eliminates the hypoxic stimulus for EPO production. Hematocrit normalizes over 3–6 months at sea level. This is often impractical but represents the definitive treatment for high-altitude polycythemia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation
alternativeTreatments.description: Smoking cessation allows normalization of carboxyhemoglobin levels (which cause apparent hypoxemia) and correction of plasma volume contraction (which causes spurious polycythemia). Hemoglobin and hematocrit may return to normal within 4–8 weeks of quitting smoking.

alternativeTreatments.name: Adequate hydration
alternativeTreatments.description: Maintaining adequate hydration prevents relative polycythemia from plasma volume contraction. Patients with secondary polycythemia should drink 2–3 liters of water daily and avoid diuretics unless medically necessary.

whySeeSpecialist: A hematologist should evaluate any patient with confirmed erythrocytosis to distinguish primary (PV) from secondary polycythemia, as management and thrombotic risk differ substantially. Pulmonologists manage the underlying COPD or sleep apnea contributing to secondary polycythemia. Nephrologists may be involved in paraneoplastic cases or renal EPO-secreting conditions.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a hematologist experienced in myeloproliferative disorders and erythrocytosis differential diagnosis. Ask whether they perform JAK2 mutation testing, EPO level measurement, and iron studies as part of the initial workup. For patients with COPD or OSA contributing to polycythemia, a pulmonologist and/or sleep specialist should be co-managing the underlying condition.
preventionStrategies: Smoking cessation — prevents both true and relative polycythemia caused by smoking; also prevents COPD, the most common cause of COPD-related polycythemia
CPAP treatment for OSA — adherence to CPAP eliminates nocturnal hypoxemia and prevents the development or worsening of OSA-related polycythemia
Long-term oxygen therapy for COPD patients with chronic hypoxemia — LTOT prevents progression to severe polycythemia and reduces mortality; PaO2 monitoring guides the need for LTOT

Avoidance of iron supplementation in polycythemic patients without iron deficiency — iron repletion in polycythemic patients worsens erythrocytosis by providing the raw material for accelerated RBC production
Adequate hydration — prevents relative polycythemia from plasma volume contraction
lifestyleModifications: Smoking cessation — the single most important modifiable risk factor; eliminates smoking-induced hypoxemia and plasma volume contraction
Weight loss in OSA patients — weight reduction of 10–15% can reduce OSA severity, decrease nocturnal hypoxemia, and lower EPO and hematocrit levels
Adequate hydration — drinking 2–3 liters of water daily maintains plasma volume and prevents spurious elevation of hematocrit
Avoidance of diuretics unless medically necessary — diuretics cause plasma volume contraction, elevating hematocrit; discuss alternatives with your physician
Avoidance of high-altitude environments — for patients with severe high-altitude polycythemia, avoiding altitudes above 2,500 meters prevents worsening of erythrocytosis

recommended: Low-iron diet if polycythemia is severe — avoiding red meat, iron-fortified cereals, and iron supplements reduces the iron available for accelerated red cell production; important in conjunction with phlebotomy to prevent rebound erythrocytosis
High-fiber diet — constipation is common in polycythemic patients, possibly related to hyperviscosity affecting GI motility; fiber supplements and adequate hydration help prevent constipation
Antioxidant-rich foods — fruits and vegetables provide antioxidants that may protect against vascular complications of polycythemia; particularly important for patients with concurrent COPD
Adequate protein intake — supports overall health in patients with chronic illness; lean meats, fish, legumes
avoid: Iron supplements — iron repletion worsens erythrocytosis in polycythemic patients by providing the iron needed for new RBC production; iron supplements should not be taken unless iron deficiency is confirmed and polycythemia has been adequately controlled
High-iron foods in excess (red meat, organ meats) — excessive red meat consumption provides heme iron that can support accelerated erythropoiesis; moderation is advised in severe polycythemia
Dehydration — inadequate fluid intake concentrates the blood, elevating hematocrit and worsening hyperviscosity; 2–3 liters of water daily is recommended
Excessive alcohol — alcohol can worsen hypoxemia in COPD patients and interferes with sleep architecture in OSA patients; alcohol also contributes to dehydration

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate exercise is beneficial in secondary polycythemia — regular aerobic exercise improves cardiovascular fitness, reduces dyspnea, and may reduce the inflammatory state associated with COPD. However, exercise should be tailored to the patient's cardiopulmonary fitness — high-intensity exercise may be limited by underlying lung disease. Patients with very high hematocrit (above 60%) should avoid strenuous exercise due to increased thrombotic risk and reduced oxygen delivery paradox — phlebotomy to reduce hematocrit first allows safer exercise participation.
dailyManagement: Regular monitoring of hemoglobin and hematocrit — every 3–6 months in stable patients with known secondary polycythemia; more frequent monitoring if treatment changes are made or symptoms are present
CPAP use or LTOT adherence as prescribed — adherence to treatment for the underlying condition (OSA, COPD) is the most effective way to control polycythemia long-term
Hydration monitoring — maintaining adequate fluid intake (2–3 liters daily) prevents plasma volume contraction and spurious elevation of hematocrit
Recognition of hyperviscosity symptoms — reporting persistent headache, dizziness, visual changes, or nosebleeds to the physician prompts evaluation of hematocrit and consideration of phlebotomy
Avoiding iron supplements unless iron deficiency is confirmed — routine iron supplementation in polycythemic patients can worsen erythrocytosis
Regular follow-up with hematologist and pulmonologist/sleep specialist — coordinate care for both the polycythemia and the underlying condition
prognosis: The prognosis of secondary polycythemia depends almost entirely on the underlying cause. Patients with correctable underlying conditions (OSA with CPAP adherence, smoking cessation, renal tumor resection) have an excellent prognosis — hematocrit normalizes and symptoms resolve with appropriate treatment. Patients with uncorrectable underlying conditions (severe COPD, cyanotic heart disease, high-altitude residence) can be managed effectively with phlebotomy and treatment of the underlying condition to minimize symptoms, but the polycythemia may persist for life. The thrombotic risk in secondary polycythemia, unlike in PV, is driven by the underlying condition (COPD, cyanotic heart disease) rather than by the polycythemia itself — while severe polycythemia (hematocrit above 60%) does increase thrombotic risk, it is lower than in PV at comparable hematocrit levels. Long-term survival in COPD-related polycythemia is determined by the COPD severity and the adequacy of oxygen therapy, not by the polycythemia per se.
recoveryTimeline: With treatment of the underlying cause, hematocrit typically falls by 2–5% over 3–6 months. CPAP treatment for OSA reduces EPO levels and hematocrit within 4–12 weeks. Smoking cessation leads to gradual normalization over 4–8 weeks as carboxyhemoglobin falls and plasma volume normalizes. Renal tumor resection leads to resolution within 2–3 months. LTOT in COPD patients reduces hematocrit gradually over 6–12 months. Phlebotomy produces immediate reduction in hematocrit with symptom relief within hours to days; iron restriction between phlebotomy sessions prevents rebound erythrocytosis.
complications: Hyperviscosity symptoms (headache, dizziness, visual disturbances) — elevated hematocrit increases blood viscosity, reducing microcirculatory flow and causing tissue hypoperfusion; the primary complication driving the need for phlebotomy in symptomatic patients

Thrombotic complications (stroke, MI, DVT) — while thrombotic risk in secondary polycythemia is lower than in PV, severe polycythemia (hematocrit above 60%) does increase thrombotic risk; the underlying condition (COPD, cyanotic heart disease) also independently increases thrombotic risk through inflammation and hemodynamic changes

Paradoxical bleeding — in some polycythemic patients, hyperviscosity causes platelet dysfunction and acquired von Willebrand disease, leading to bleeding tendency (epistaxis, GI bleeding) despite elevated platelet counts; phlebotomy to reduce hematocrit often improves coagulopathy

Gout from high cell turnover — accelerated erythropoiesis increases purine turnover and uric acid production; polycythemic patients are at elevated risk for gout, particularly those with high iron stores

Iron deficiency from repeated phlebotomy — therapeutic phlebotomy without iron supplementation can deplete iron stores over time, leading to iron deficiency anemia; monitoring iron studies and replacing iron when ferritin falls below 20 ng/mL is important

Post-phlebotomy rebound erythrocytosis — if iron stores are replete, phlebotomy can stimulate a rebound increase in RBC production once iron becomes available again; iron restriction between phlebotomy sessions prevents this rebound

supportResources.name: American Lung Association — COPD Management
supportResources.description: Educational resources on COPD management, including long-term oxygen therapy and smoking cessation support.

supportResources.name: MRLF (Myeloproliferative Neoplasm Research Foundation) — Polycythemia Vera Information
supportResources.description: Resources for understanding polycythemia vera and its distinction from secondary polycythemia, with patient support programs.

supportResources.name: American Sleep Apnea Association
supportResources.description: Information on sleep apnea diagnosis and treatment, including CPAP therapy and support resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polycythemia Vera (PV)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polycythemia vera is a primary bone marrow disorder driven by JAK2 mutations causing autonomous red cell production without EPO stimulation. EPO levels are low-suppressed in PV and elevated in secondary polycythemia. PV carries an inherent thrombotic risk from the myeloproliferative process itself; secondary polycythemia does not have this inherent risk — thrombosis risk comes from the underlying condition.

confusedWithConditions.name: Essential Thrombocythemia (ET)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ET is characterized by isolated thrombocytosis (platelet count above 450,000/µL) with normal or only mildly elevated hemoglobin/hematocrit. In secondary polycythemia, the elevated parameter is the red cell line, not platelets. ET is also JAK2/CALR/MPL-mutated and carries thrombotic risk from the primary myeloproliferative process.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: COPD is the underlying condition causing secondary polycythemia in many patients — it causes chronic hypoxemia, which drives EPO production and erythrocytosis. The distinction is that COPD is the primary diagnosis and polycythemia is a secondary complication; treatment of COPD (with LTOT, bronchodilators, smoking cessation) addresses the polycythemia at its source.

relatedConditions.name: Polycythemia Vera (PV)
relatedConditions.relevance: PV and secondary polycythemia both present with elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit, but the pathophysiology, treatment, and prognosis differ critically. Distinguishing PV from secondary polycythemia is the most important diagnostic step — JAK2 mutation testing and EPO level measurement are the key tools. Misdiagnosis of secondary polycythemia as PV leads to unnecessary treatment with phlebotomy targets that are too low and failure to address the underlying cause.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
relatedConditions.relevance: COPD is the most common cause of secondary polycythemia in developed countries. Managing COPD effectively — with bronchodilators, inhaled corticosteroids, pulmonary rehabilitation, smoking cessation, and LTOT — is the most effective way to control polycythemia in these patients.

relatedConditions.name: Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
relatedConditions.relevance: OSA is a common and underdiagnosed cause of secondary polycythemia. CPAP treatment eliminates nocturnal hypoxemia, normalizes EPO levels, and reduces hematocrit in most patients. OSA should be suspected in any patient with polycythemia who is obese, snores, or has excessive daytime somnolence.

faqs.question: What is secondary polycythemia?
faqs.answer: Secondary polycythemia is an elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit caused by increased erythropoietin (EPO) production in response to chronic tissue hypoxia or EPO-secreting tumors. Unlike polycythemia vera (a primary bone marrow disorder), secondary polycythemia is driven by an external cause — the most common being chronic hypoxemia from COPD, sleep apnea, or high altitude. The thrombotic risk comes from the underlying condition, not from the polycythemia itself.

faqs.question: How does secondary polycythemia differ from polycythemia vera?
faqs.answer: Secondary polycythemia is driven by elevated EPO (from hypoxia or tumors), while polycythemia vera is driven by JAK2 mutations causing autonomous EPO-independent red cell production. PV has low/suppressed EPO; secondary polycythemia has elevated EPO. PV carries an inherent thrombotic risk from the myeloproliferative process; secondary polycythemia does not. Treatment differs substantially — PV is treated with phlebotomy and hydroxyurea; secondary polycythemia is treated by managing the underlying cause.

faqs.question: What causes secondary polycythemia?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are chronic hypoxemia from COPD (low oxygen from damaged lungs), obstructive sleep apnea (repeated nocturnal oxygen drops), and high-altitude residence. Rare causes include EPO-secreting tumors (renal cell carcinoma, HCC), cyanotic heart disease, and congenital mutations in the hypoxia-sensing pathway (VHL, PHD2). Smoking also causes a form of polycythemia through plasma volume contraction and carboxyhemoglobin-induced hypoxia.

faqs.question: How is secondary polycythemia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis involves confirming elevated hemoglobin/hematocrit, ruling out PV with JAK2 mutation testing (negative in secondary polycythemia), and measuring EPO levels (elevated in secondary polycythemia, suppressed in PV). Additional tests for the underlying cause include arterial blood gases, sleep study, chest imaging, and abdominal imaging for tumor evaluation.

faqs.question: How is secondary polycythemia treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment focuses on managing the underlying cause — LTOT for COPD, CPAP for sleep apnea, smoking cessation, tumor resection for paraneoplastic cases, or relocation from high altitude. Phlebotomy is used for symptomatic hyperviscosity or hematocrit above 55–60%. Iron supplements should be avoided unless iron deficiency is documented.

faqs.question: Is secondary polycythemia dangerous?
faqs.answer: The polycythemia itself is generally less dangerous than in polycythemia vera — the thrombotic risk comes from the underlying condition (COPD, OSA) rather than the elevated blood counts. However, very high hematocrit (above 60%) can cause hyperviscosity symptoms and modestly increase thrombotic risk. Managing the underlying condition is more important for reducing overall risk than treating the polycythemia per se.

faqs.question: Can secondary polycythemia be cured?
faqs.answer: If the underlying cause is corrected, secondary polycythemia resolves completely — CPAP for sleep apnea, smoking cessation, renal tumor resection, or LTOT for COPD all reduce EPO and normalize hematocrit. Conditions that cannot be fully corrected (advanced COPD, cyanotic heart disease, high-altitude residence) require ongoing management to keep hematocrit in a safe range.

faqs.question: What is the target hematocrit for secondary polycythemia?
faqs.answer: The target hematocrit in secondary polycythemia is typically 45–50% — slightly higher than in PV because the thrombotic risk is lower. Phlebotomy is generally reserved for symptomatic hyperviscosity (headache, dizziness, visual changes) or pre-operative preparation. Lower targets are not typically necessary and may cause symptomatic anemia.

faqs.question: Why should iron supplements be avoided in secondary polycythemia?
faqs.answer: Iron supplements provide the raw material for accelerated red blood cell production. In a patient with secondary polycythemia, giving iron can worsen erythrocytosis by allowing more red cells to be produced. Iron restriction between phlebotomy sessions also prevents rebound erythrocytosis — if iron stores are adequate, phlebotomy stimulates EPO-driven replacement of removed red cells faster than if iron stores are depleted.

faqs.question: Does secondary polycythemia increase stroke risk?
faqs.answer: Very high hematocrit (above 60%) does increase thrombotic risk, including stroke, particularly in cyanotic heart disease. However, the stroke risk in secondary polycythemia is primarily driven by the underlying condition (COPD with hypoxemia, cyanotic heart disease) rather than by the polycythemia alone. Management focuses on both the underlying condition and reducing hematocrit to safe levels when symptoms warrant.

faqs.question: What is the role of EPO level in diagnosing secondary polycythemia?
faqs.answer: Serum EPO level is central to distinguishing primary from secondary polycythemia. In secondary polycythemia, EPO is elevated (appropriate response to hypoxia) or very high (in paraneoplastic secretion from tumors). In polycythemia vera, EPO is suppressed (low or below-normal). A normal EPO in a patient with significant erythrocytosis is actually inappropriately low and suggests primary rather than secondary polycythemia.

faqs.question: Can sleep apnea cause polycythemia?
faqs.answer: Yes — obstructive sleep apnea is one of the most common causes of secondary polycythemia. Repeated episodes of nocturnal oxygen desaturation stimulate EPO production, leading to erythrocytosis over months to years. CPAP treatment eliminates nocturnal hypoxemia, normalizes EPO levels, and reduces hematocrit in most patients within 4–12 weeks.

faqs.question: Is high-altitude polycythemia dangerous?
faqs.answer: High-altitude polycythemia (excess erythrocytosis) can cause symptoms of hyperviscosity (headache, dizziness, fatigue) and may increase thrombotic risk when severe (hemoglobin above 21 g/dL). Tibetan highlanders have a genetic adaptation that prevents extreme polycythemia; Andean populations may develop more severe polycythemia. Relocation to lower altitude is the definitive treatment.

faqs.question: What is Chuvash polycythemia?
faqs.answer: Chuvash polycythemia is a congenital erythrocytosis caused by a VHL gene mutation (VHL 598C>T), most common in the Chuvash population of Russia. It causes elevated EPO and erythrocytosis without chronic hypoxemia. Patients with Chuvash polycythemia have increased thrombotic risk and require careful management.

faqs.question: Does secondary polycythemia run in families?
faqs.answer: Secondary polycythemia as a consequence of COPD, sleep apnea, or high-altitude exposure is not directly inherited. However, congenital erythrocytosis syndromes (VHL, PHD2, EPOR mutations) are inherited and can cause polycythemia in multiple family members. Familial clustering of COPD or sleep apnea (which have genetic and environmental components) can create the appearance of familial polycythemia through shared risk for the underlying conditions.

faqs.question: What blood tests are needed for secondary polycythemia workup?
faqs.answer: Key tests include: CBC with hemoglobin and hematocrit, JAK2 mutation testing (to rule out PV), serum EPO level, arterial blood gases, and iron studies (ferritin, TIBC). Additional tests depend on the suspected cause: sleep study for OSA, chest imaging for COPD, abdominal imaging for tumor evaluation. Carboxyhemoglobin is useful in smokers to quantify the contribution of smoking to apparent hypoxemia.

faqs.question: Can dehydration cause polycythemia?
faqs.answer: Dehydration causes relative or spurious polycythemia — plasma volume contraction concentrates red cells, raising measured hemoglobin and hematocrit without increasing the actual red cell mass. This is distinguished from true secondary polycythemia by normal red cell mass on radioisotope studies or by correcting with rehydration. Relative polycythemia resolves with adequate hydration and is not associated with the same thrombotic risk as true polycythemia.

faqs.question: How often should hematocrit be monitored in secondary polycythemia?
faqs.answer: In stable patients with known secondary polycythemia and a managed underlying condition, hemoglobin and hematocrit should be checked every 3–6 months. More frequent monitoring is needed when treatment changes are made (e.g., starting LTOT, initiating CPAP) or after phlebotomy sessions. Patients with unstable underlying conditions (frequently exacerbating COPD, poorly controlled OSA) may need more frequent monitoring.

faqs.question: Is polycythemia more common in men or women?
faqs.answer: Polycythemia is more common in men, with a male-to-female ratio of approximately 2:1. This is explained by differences in baseline hemoglobin (men have higher hemoglobin than women), higher testosterone levels stimulating erythropoiesis in men, and higher rates of COPD and smoking-related disease in men. Estrogen and menstrual iron loss in premenopausal women provide some protection.

faqs.question: What is aquagenic pruritus in polycythemia?
faqs.answer: Aquagenic pruritus is itching after bathing — it is classically associated with polycythemia vera and is thought to be related to histamine release from increased basophils. It can occur in secondary polycythemia as well, though less commonly. The symptom is not diagnostic of PV versus secondary polycythemia but is a useful clue pointing toward a myeloproliferative disorder when present.

metaTitle: Secondary Polycythemia: Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment |
metaDescription: Secondary polycythemia: causes (COPD, sleep apnea, high altitude, EPO-secreting tumors), diagnosis (JAK2 testing, EPO levels), treatment, and how it differs
